PART B: Customer Segmentation with Unsupervised LearningÂ¶

Name: Ng Yu Hang
Admin Number: [student-id]
Class: DAAA/FT/1B/07

Project ObjectiveÂ¶

Background:
We collected information from 200 customers, including their Gender, Age, Income, and How Much They Spend.
The goal is to group customers into clusters to better understand their needs and create targeted strategies.

Dataset:
You are going to use the dataset: CA2-Customer-Data.csv, which contains:

  • Gender
  • Age
  • Income (k$)
  • How Much They Spend

Tasks:Â¶

1. Clustering Task:
Write the code to solve the clustering task. Use the scikit-learn library to build the model.
Note: Do not use other autoML libraries.

2. Jupyter Notebook Report:
In the Jupyter notebook, write your report detailing your implementation, your experiments, and analysis.
Include your Python code with comments.

3. Hyperparameter Tuning:
Test your clustering with different possible values of k.
Determine the best possible value of k, and show how you can determine that this is the best value.

4. Multiple Clustering Models:
Use more than just one clustering algorithm (e.g., K-Means, DBSCAN, Agglomerative Clustering, GMM).
Compare their results and effectiveness.

5. Model Evaluation:
Analyze the clusters and describe the characteristics of each customer group.
Identify which group of customers needs more attention and explain why.

6. Presentation Slides:
Create a set of slides summarizing your Jupyter notebook report.

  • Explain the entire machine learning process you went through:
    • Data exploration, data cleaning, feature engineering, model building, model evaluation, and model improvement.
  • Write your conclusions.
  • The slides should not exceed 20 pages.

PhasesÂ¶

The project is divided into the following 6 phases:

  1. Data Collection and Preprocessing
  • The dataset is loaded and cleaned by handling missing values, duplicates, and outliers. Feature engineering is performed, and numerical features are standardized. Dimensionality reduction techniques such as PCA and t-SNE are applied for better visualization and clustering efficiency.
  1. Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)
  • EDA is conducted to uncover patterns, trends, and relationships within the data. Visualizations such as histograms, scatter plots, and heatmaps are used to analyze feature distributions and correlations. Insights from this phase guide feature selection for clustering.
  1. Implementing Multiple Clustering Models
  • Four clustering models are implemented: K-Means, Agglomerative, DBSCAN, and GMM. The optimal number of clusters is determined using methods such as the Elbow Method, Silhouette Score, and Dendrogram analysis. Model performance is evaluated using clustering metrics.
  1. Clustering Model Comparison & Final Insights
  • The performance of all clustering models is compared using key metrics such as Silhouette Score, Davies-Bouldin Index, and Calinski-Harabasz Score. Visual comparisons of cluster formations help identify the most suitable model for customer segmentation.
  1. Business Insights & Final Conclusions
  • Customer segments are profiled based on cluster characteristics such as age, income, spending patterns, and efficiency scores. Key insights are derived to suggest targeted marketing strategies and customer engagement plans for each segment.
  1. Answering Business Questions with Visualizations
  • Key business questions are addressed through visualizations that explain customer behavior across segments. Plots such as income vs. spending and age vs. spending are used to identify high-value customers, budget-conscious consumers, and potential growth segments.

Importing Necessary Libraries ðŸ“šÂ¶

Before diving into the analysis, we'll import the necessary libraries for data manipulation, visualization, modeling, and evaluation.

InÂ [79]:
# Data Manipulation and Numerical Operations
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Visualization
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# Warnings Management
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

# Statistical Analysis
from scipy.stats.mstats import winsorize

# Machine Learning - Preprocessing and Dimensionality Reduction
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
from sklearn.manifold import TSNE

# Machine Learning - Clustering Models
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans, AgglomerativeClustering, DBSCAN
from sklearn.mixture import GaussianMixture

# Machine Learning - Clustering Metrics
from sklearn.metrics import (
    silhouette_score,
    davies_bouldin_score,
    calinski_harabasz_score
)

# Hierarchical Clustering Tools
import scipy.cluster.hierarchy as sch

# Nearest Neighbors for DBSCAN Epsilon Estimation
from sklearn.neighbors import NearestNeighbors

Phase 1: Data Collection and PreprocessingÂ¶

In this phase, we'll cover the following key processes to ensure our dataset is clean, consistent, and ready for clustering models:

Data Loading and Initial InspectionÂ¶

  • Loading the CA2-Customer-Data.csv file into a Pandas DataFrame.
  • Inspecting the dataset using .head(), .info(), and .describe() to understand its structure and summary statistics.

Data CleaningÂ¶

  • Handling Missing Values: Checking for and addressing any missing values using appropriate imputation techniques.
  • Removing Duplicates: Identifying and removing duplicate records to ensure data integrity.
  • Dropping Irrelevant Columns: Excluding non-contributory features such as CustomerID.

Feature EngineeringÂ¶

  • Categorical Encoding: Converting Gender into numerical values (Male â†’ 0, Female â†’ 1).
  • Derived Features: Creating new metrics such as Spending Efficiency (Spending / Income).

Outlier Detection and TreatmentÂ¶

  • Detecting Outliers: Using Z-score and IQR methods to identify extreme values.
  • Treating Outliers: Applying Winsorization to cap outliers and maintain data consistency.

Feature ScalingÂ¶

  • Standardization: Using StandardScaler to normalize numerical features, ensuring equal importance during clustering.

Dimensionality ReductionÂ¶

  • PCA (Principal Component Analysis): Reducing feature dimensions while retaining variance for better model efficiency.
  • t-SNE (t-Distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding): Visualizing potential clusters by mapping high-dimensional data to 2D.

Outcome:
By the end of this phase, the dataset will be fully cleaned, enriched with new features, and standardized, ensuring it is ready for effective customer segmentation through clustering models.

Phase 1.2: Handling Missing Values, Duplicates & Data CleaningÂ¶

ObjectiveÂ¶

Now that we have inspected the dataset, we will:
Drop unnecessary columns (CustomerID is not needed for clustering).
Handle categorical variables (Convert Gender into numerical values).
Detect and remove outliers to prevent skewed clustering.

StepsÂ¶

  1. Drop CustomerID Column
    • CustomerID is a unique identifier and holds no meaningful value for clustering.
  2. Convert Categorical Features
    • The Gender column will be mapped to numerical values (Male â†’ 0, Female â†’ 1).
  3. Outlier Detection & Treatment
    • Outliers can distort clustering results.
    • We will use Z-score and Interquartile Range (IQR) methods to detect extreme values.
    • Any detected outliers will be reviewed before removal.
InÂ [Â ]:
# Drop unnecessary columns
df = df.drop(columns=["CustomerID"])

# Convert categorical feature "Gender" to numerical
df["Gender"] = df["Gender"].map({"Male": 0, "Female": 1})

# Function to detect outliers using Z-score
def detect_outliers_zscore(data, threshold=3):
    z_scores = np.abs((data - data.mean()) / data.std())
    return z_scores > threshold

# Function to detect outliers using IQR
def detect_outliers_iqr(data):
    Q1 = data.quantile(0.25)
    Q3 = data.quantile(0.75)
    IQR = Q3 - Q1
    return (data < (Q1 - 1.5 * IQR)) | (data > (Q3 + 1.5 * IQR))

# Apply both outlier detection methods
zscore_outliers = df.apply(detect_outliers_zscore)
iqr_outliers = df.apply(detect_outliers_iqr)

# Combine results
outlier_counts = (zscore_outliers | iqr_outliers).sum()

# Display number of detected outliers per column
print("Outlier Count Per Feature:")
print(outlier_counts)

# Set the style for better aesthetics
sns.set_style("whitegrid")

# Create a more visually appealing boxplot
plt.figure(figsize=(14, 6))
palette = sns.color_palette("coolwarm", len(df.columns))

for i, column in enumerate(df.columns):
    plt.subplot(1, 4, i + 1)
    
    # Enhanced boxplot with advanced styling
    sns.boxplot(
        y=df[column],
        color=palette[i],
        width=0.6,
        linewidth=2.5,
        fliersize=5,  # Adjust size of outlier points
        medianprops={"color": "black", "linewidth": 2},  # Styling median line
        whiskerprops={"linewidth": 2, "color": "gray"},  # Styling whiskers
        capprops={"linewidth": 2, "color": "black"},  # Styling caps
        boxprops={"facecolor": palette[i], "edgecolor": "black", "linewidth": 2},  # Styling box
    )

    plt.title(column, fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
    plt.ylabel("")  # Remove y-axis label for a cleaner look

# Overall plot settings
plt.suptitle(" Outlier Detection - Boxplot Analysis", fontsize=16, fontweight="bold")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

ðŸš¨ Outlier Count Per Feature:

Age                    0
Income (k$)            2
How Much They Spend    0
dtype: int64

Overview of the distribution and spread of key numerical features.

Key Insights:Â¶

  • Gender: Only two categories (Male = 0, Female = 1), no anomalies.
  • Age: Even distribution from 18 to 70, no significant outliers.
  • Income: One outlier above 130k, which may represent a high-net-worth individual.
  • Spending: Broad range from 1 to 99, with no apparent extreme outliers.

Data Quality Check:Â¶

  • Outliers appear minimal, indicating the data is well-prepared.
  • No need for further outlier removal.

Phase 1.3: Handling Outliers & Feature EngineeringÂ¶

ObjectiveÂ¶

Now that we have detected outliers using Z-score and IQR methods, we will:
Decide whether to remove or cap extreme values based on business logic.
Create new derived features to enhance clustering relevance.
Prepare data for scaling and transformation.

StepsÂ¶

  1. Outlier Treatment
    • Outliers in Income might indicate high-net-worth individuals, which could be valid.
    • Instead of removing, we cap extreme values using the Winsorization technique.
  2. Feature Engineering
    • Spending Efficiency Score = How Much They Spend / Income
    • Age-Income Ratio = Income / Age
  3. Final Data Preparation
    • Store the cleaned and feature-engineered dataset for further processing.
InÂ [83]:
# Winsorization: Capping extreme values
df["Income (k$)"] = winsorize(df["Income (k$)"], limits=[0.01, 0.01])  # Cap top & bottom 1%
df["How Much They Spend"] = winsorize(df["How Much They Spend"], limits=[0.01, 0.01])

# Feature Engineering - Creating new derived features
df["Spending Efficiency"] = df["How Much They Spend"] / df["Income (k$)"]
df["Age-Income Ratio"] = df["Income (k$)"] / df["Age"]

# Display dataset after processing
print("\n Updated Dataset After Feature Engineering:")
df.head()

 Updated Dataset After Feature Engineering:
Out[83]:
Gender Age Income (k$) How Much They Spend Spending Efficiency Age-Income Ratio

1 0 21 16 81 5.062500 0.761905
2 1 20 16 6 0.375000 0.800000

Phase 1.4: Feature Scaling & Dimensionality ReductionÂ¶

ObjectiveÂ¶

Now that our dataset is cleaned and feature-engineered, we need to:
Normalize numerical features to ensure uniform impact on clustering.
Use PCA (Principal Component Analysis) for dimensionality reduction.
Apply t-SNE for visualizing potential clusters.

StepsÂ¶

  1. Scaling the Features
    • Use StandardScaler to standardize data (mean = 0, variance = 1).
    • Certain clustering models (e.g., K-Means, DBSCAN) are sensitive to scale differences.
  2. Dimensionality Reduction (PCA & t-SNE)
    • PCA: Reduce feature dimensions while retaining 95% variance.
    • t-SNE: Map high-dimensional data into 2D space for better visualization.
InÂ [Â ]:
# Select features for scaling
features_to_scale = ["Age", "Income (k$)", "How Much They Spend", "Spending Efficiency", "Age-Income Ratio"]

# Apply Standard Scaling
scaler = StandardScaler()
df_scaled = df.copy()
df_scaled[features_to_scale] = scaler.fit_transform(df[features_to_scale])

# Apply PCA for dimensionality reduction
pca = PCA(n_components=2)  # Reduce to 2 dimensions for visualization
df_pca = pca.fit_transform(df_scaled[features_to_scale])

# Convert PCA results into a DataFrame
df_pca = pd.DataFrame(df_pca, columns=["PCA1", "PCA2"])
df_pca["Gender"] = df["Gender"]  # Retain categorical variable for reference

# Apply t-SNE for 2D visualization
tsne = TSNE(n_components=2, perplexity=30, random_state=42)
df_tsne = tsne.fit_transform(df_scaled[features_to_scale])

# Convert t-SNE results into a DataFrame
df_tsne = pd.DataFrame(df_tsne, columns=["tSNE1", "tSNE2"])
df_tsne["Gender"] = df["Gender"]

# Display transformed datasets
print("\nPCA Reduced Dataset:")
print(df_pca.head())

print("\nt-SNE Transformed Dataset:")
print(df_tsne.head())

# Define a distinct color palette for better visibility
color_palette = "Set1"  # A high-contrast color palette

#  PCA Visualization with New Colors
plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))
sns.scatterplot(
    x=df_pca["PCA1"], 
    y=df_pca["PCA2"], 
    hue=df_pca["Gender"], 
    palette=color_palette, 
    alpha=0.75, 
    edgecolor="black"
)
plt.xlabel("PCA Component 1", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
plt.ylabel("PCA Component 2", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
plt.title("PCA Projection of Customer Data", fontsize=14, fontweight="bold")
plt.legend(title="Gender")
plt.grid(True, linestyle="--", alpha=0.5)
plt.show()

#  t-SNE Visualization with New Colors
plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))
sns.scatterplot(
    x=df_tsne["tSNE1"], 
    y=df_tsne["tSNE2"], 
    hue=df_tsne["Gender"], 
    palette=color_palette, 
    alpha=0.75, 
    edgecolor="black"
)
plt.xlabel("t-SNE Component 1", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
plt.ylabel("t-SNE Component 2", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
plt.title("t-SNE Projection of Customer Data", fontsize=14, fontweight="bold")
plt.legend(title="Gender")
plt.grid(True, linestyle="--", alpha=0.5)
plt.show()

PCA Reduced Dataset:
       PCA1      PCA2  Gender
0  2.127096 -0.779575       0
1  3.821184 -2.766737       0
2  0.879225  0.932429       1
3  3.721171 -2.455149       1
4  2.332363 -0.138528       1

t-SNE Transformed Dataset:
      tSNE1     tSNE2  Gender
0  1.515202 -7.657561       0
1  5.101877 -9.184182       0
2 -2.617802 -9.565855       1
3  4.862892 -9.036869       1
4  0.918088 -7.482295       1

Dimensionality Reduction Analysis (PCA & t-SNE)Â¶

PCA Projection:Â¶

  • Less separation between clusters: PCA shows that customers are overlapping across both components.
  • Gender distribution is mixed: No clear grouping based on gender alone.
  • Linear patterns: PCA is useful for linear relationships but may miss complex structures.

t-SNE Projection:Â¶

  • Distinct cluster formations: Clear grouping patterns, indicating potential customer segments.
  • Better separation than PCA: Suitable for capturing non-linear patterns.
  • Gender still not a key differentiator: Similar distribution across clusters.

Insight: t-SNE is more effective for visualizing potential clusters, highlighting the need for clustering models that handle non-linear structures.

Phase 2: Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)Â¶

In this phase, we explore the dataset to uncover patterns, relationships, and insights that will guide the clustering process. EDA is crucial for understanding the data's structure and distribution. The following tasks are performed:

  • Statistical Summary:
    • Examine summary statistics using .describe() to understand the central tendency and spread of each feature.
    • Analyze distributions of numerical columns (Age, Income, How Much They Spend).
  • Univariate Analysis:
    • Plot histograms and boxplots to visualize the distribution and identify potential outliers.
    • Analyze categorical feature distributions, such as Gender.
  • Bivariate Analysis:
    • Use scatter plots to explore relationships between Income and Spending.
    • Analyze pairwise relationships using pair plots or correlation heatmaps.
  • Multivariate Analysis:
    • Create pair plots to examine the relationship between multiple numerical features.
    • Generate a correlation heatmap to identify relationships and dependencies between variables.
  • Outlier Analysis:
    • Identify outliers using boxplots and address them using capping techniques or Winsorization.
  • Feature Relationships:
    • Analyze how Age, Income, and Spending interact and identify trends or clusters visually.
  • Gender-Based Insights:
    • Compare spending patterns and income distribution across genders using bar plots or violin plots.

This EDA phase helps us gain valuable insights into customer behavior, which informs our clustering model selection and parameter tuning.

InÂ [85]:
# 2.2.1 Histograms for Numerical Features
numeric_columns = df.select_dtypes(include=["int64", "float64"]).columns

plt.figure(figsize=(14, 8))
colors = ["#4CAF50", "#FF9800", "#2196F3", "#9C27B0", "#FFC107"]

for i, column in enumerate(numeric_columns, 1):
    plt.subplot(2, 3, i)
    plt.hist(df[column], bins=25, edgecolor="black", color=colors[i % len(colors)])
    plt.title(f"{column}", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
    plt.xlabel(column, fontsize=10)
    plt.ylabel("Frequency", fontsize=10)
    plt.grid(True, linestyle="--", alpha=0.7)

plt.suptitle(" Histograms for Numerical Features", fontsize=16, fontweight="bold")
plt.tight_layout(pad=3.0)
plt.show()

Histograms for Numerical Features AnalysisÂ¶

The histograms provide a distribution overview of numerical features, helping us understand patterns, skewness, and data concentration.

Gender:Â¶

  • Balanced distribution of genders (Male: 0, Female: 1).

Age:Â¶

  • Majority of customers are aged between 20 to 50 years.
  • The age distribution is right-skewed, with fewer customers above 60.

Income (k$):Â¶

  • Income is bimodal, with peaks around 60k and 80k.
  • A few high-income outliers are present.

How Much They Spend:Â¶

  • Spending shows a wide spread with peaks between 40 to 60 units.
  • Some customers are low spenders, while others reach near 100 units.

Spending Efficiency:Â¶

  • Highly right-skewed with most values between 0.5 and 1.5.
  • A few customers have an efficiency above 3, indicating low income but high spending behavior.

Age-Income Ratio:Â¶

  • Right-skewed with most values under 2.5, indicating younger customers have a higher income-to-age ratio.
  • A long tail suggests a small group of older high-income individuals.

ðŸ“ Insights:Â¶

  • Age and income distributions indicate a mix of customer demographics.
  • Spending patterns are diverse, supporting potential cluster formations.
  • Spending efficiency and age-income ratio are likely strong segmentation features.
InÂ [86]:
# 2.3.2 Correlation Heatmap
plt.figure(figsize=(7, 5))
corr = df.corr()
sns.heatmap(
    corr, 
    annot=True, 
    cmap="coolwarm", 
    linewidths=0.5, 
    fmt=".2f"
)
plt.title("Correlation Heatmap", fontsize=13, fontweight="bold")
plt.show()

Correlation Heatmap AnalysisÂ¶

The heatmap displays correlation coefficients between numerical features. Values close to 1 or -1 indicate strong correlations, while values near 0 show weak or no relationship.

Strong Correlations:Â¶

  • Income (k$) and Age-Income Ratio (0.76): Higher income results in a higher age-income ratio, highlighting income as a key factor for segmentation.
  • Spending Efficiency and How Much They Spend (0.60): Customers who spend more tend to have higher spending efficiency (spending relative to income).

Moderate to Weak Correlations:Â¶

  • Age and Age-Income Ratio (-0.57): Older individuals show a lower income-to-age ratio.
  • Income and Spending Efficiency (-0.57): Higher-income customers spend a smaller proportion of their earnings.

Minimal Correlations:Â¶

  • Gender: No significant impact on income, spending, or efficiency.
  • Age and Spending (-0.33): Younger customers tend to spend more, but the relationship is weak.

ðŸ“ Insights for Clustering:Â¶

  • Income, Spending Efficiency, and Age-Income Ratio are highly valuable for cluster separation.
  • Spending behavior and efficiency patterns will help differentiate high spenders from budget-conscious customers.
  • Age and spending habits could form generational customer segments.
InÂ [87]:
# Create Pair Plot with Explicit Labels
pairplot = sns.pairplot(df, hue="Gender", palette={0: "red", 1: "blue"}, diag_kind="kde")
pairplot._legend.set_title("Gender")
new_labels = ["Male (0)", "Female (1)"]
for t, l in zip(pairplot._legend.texts, new_labels):
    t.set_text(l)

plt.suptitle("Pair Plot for Numerical Features by Gender", fontsize=14, fontweight="bold")
plt.show()

Pair Plot Analysis for Numerical FeaturesÂ¶

This pair plot displays the relationships between Age, Income, and Spending, with points colored by Gender (0 = Male, 1 = Female).

** Key Observations:**Â¶

1. Age Distribution:

  • There are more customers aged 30 to 50, as shown by the density peaks.
  • The age distribution for both genders is similar, though males (blue) are slightly more concentrated in their 50s.

2. Income Distribution:

  • Income is normally distributed, peaking between 40k to 80k.
  • Males (blue) show a slightly higher income range than females (red).

3. Spending Distribution:

  • Most customers spend between 20 to 80k, with a small group spending above 100k.
  • Females (red) have a wider range of spending habits.

4. Age vs. Income:

  • Younger customers (20-40) show a wide range of incomes, suggesting a diverse customer base (e.g., young professionals and students).
  • Middle-aged customers (40-60) cluster tightly around 50k to 90k income, showing financial stability.

5. Income vs. Spending:

  • There is no clear linear relationship; some high-income customers spend less (potential savers).
  • A group of low-income customers spends heavily, indicating possible over-spending behavior.

6. Age vs. Spending:

  • Younger customers (20-35) are more likely to be high spenders, supporting the idea that younger demographics are driven by lifestyle and experiences.
  • Older customers (50+) show lower spending trends, possibly due to retirement or conservative habits.

** Insights for Clustering:**Â¶

  • Age and Income are likely to form distinct segments such as young high-spenders, middle-aged stable earners, and older conservative spenders.
  • Gender alone does not significantly separate customer behavior, but it may help fine-tune targeted marketing campaigns.
  • Income vs. Spending efficiency will be crucial to identify luxury buyers vs. budget-conscious customers.
InÂ [Â ]:
# 2.5 Spending Distribution by Gender (Violin Plot)
plt.figure(figsize=(7, 5))
sns.violinplot(
    x="Gender", 
    y="How Much They Spend", 
    data=df, 
    palette="Set3"
)
plt.title(" Spending Distribution by Gender", fontsize=13, fontweight="bold")
plt.xlabel("Gender", fontsize=11)
plt.ylabel("How Much They Spend", fontsize=11)
plt.grid(True, linestyle="--", alpha=0.5)
plt.show()

Spending Distribution by Gender (Violin Plot)Â¶

The violin plot shows the distribution of customer spending by gender.

Observations:Â¶

  • Both genders have a similar median spending value.
  • Males (0): More spread-out spending patterns with some lower and higher outliers.
  • Females (1): Slightly more concentrated spending in the mid-range but with a broader tail at higher spending levels.

Insights for Clustering:Â¶

  • Gender alone may not be a strong cluster separator due to similar distributions.
  • Higher spending behavior is visible across both genders, indicating income and spending efficiency may be more valuable features.

Phase 3: Implementing Multiple Clustering ModelsÂ¶

ObjectiveÂ¶

Now that we have scaled and reduced dimensionality of our dataset, we will:
Implement multiple clustering algorithms to find the best segmentation.
Compare clustering methods to determine the most effective approach.
Evaluate clustering performance using different metrics.

StepsÂ¶

  1. K-Means Clustering (Centroid-Based)
    • Find the optimal k value using the Elbow Method & Silhouette Score.
  2. Agglomerative Clustering (Hierarchical-Based)
    • Use Dendrograms to determine the best cluster separation.
  3. DBSCAN (Density-Based Clustering)
    • Identify clusters based on density instead of predefined k.
  4. Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM) (Probabilistic Clustering)
    • Assign probability scores for each cluster membership.
  5. Cluster Validation Metrics
    • Silhouette Score (Measures compactness & separation).
    • Davies-Bouldin Index (Measures within-cluster compactness).
    • Calinski-Harabasz Index (Evaluates variance between clusters).
InÂ [Â ]:
# Determine the optimal number of clusters using the Elbow Method
wcss = []  # Within-Cluster Sum of Squares
silhouette_scores = []

# Test different values of k
k_values = range(2, 11)  # Checking k from 2 to 10
for k in k_values:
    kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=k, random_state=42, n_init=10)
    cluster_labels = kmeans.fit_predict(df_pca)
    
    wcss.append(kmeans.inertia_)
    silhouette_scores.append(silhouette_score(df_pca, cluster_labels))

#  Plot the Elbow Method
plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))
plt.plot(k_values, wcss, marker="o", linestyle="dashed", color="b")
plt.xlabel("Number of Clusters (k)", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
plt.ylabel("Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
plt.title(" Elbow Method for Optimal k", fontsize=14, fontweight="bold")
plt.xticks(k_values)
plt.grid(True, linestyle="--", alpha=0.5)
plt.show()

#  Plot the Silhouette Scores
plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))
plt.plot(k_values, silhouette_scores, marker="o", linestyle="dashed", color="g")
plt.xlabel("Number of Clusters (k)", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
plt.ylabel("Silhouette Score", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
plt.title(" Silhouette Score Analysis", fontsize=14, fontweight="bold")
plt.xticks(k_values)
plt.grid(True, linestyle="--", alpha=0.5)
plt.show()

# Select the best k (using Silhouette Score)
optimal_k = k_values[np.argmax(silhouette_scores)]
print(f" Optimal Number of Clusters Based on Silhouette Score: {optimal_k}")

# Apply K-Means Clustering with the best k
kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=optimal_k, random_state=42, n_init=10)
df_pca["KMeans_Cluster"] = kmeans.fit_predict(df_pca)

#  Visualizing K-Means Clustering Results
plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))
sns.scatterplot(
    x=df_pca["PCA1"], 
    y=df_pca["PCA2"], 
    hue=df_pca["KMeans_Cluster"], 
    palette="Set2", 
    alpha=0.8, 
    edgecolor="black"
)
plt.xlabel("PCA Component 1", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
plt.ylabel("PCA Component 2", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
plt.title(" K-Means Clustering (PCA Projection)", fontsize=14, fontweight="bold")
plt.legend(title="Cluster")
plt.grid(True, linestyle="--", alpha=0.5)
plt.show()

#  Clustering Performance Metrics
silhouette = silhouette_score(df_pca[["PCA1", "PCA2"]], df_pca["KMeans_Cluster"])
davies_bouldin = davies_bouldin_score(df_pca[["PCA1", "PCA2"]], df_pca["KMeans_Cluster"])
calinski_harabasz = calinski_harabasz_score(df_pca[["PCA1", "PCA2"]], df_pca["KMeans_Cluster"])

print(f"Silhouette Score (Higher is Better): {silhouette:.4f}")
print(f"Davies-Bouldin Score (Lower is Better): {davies_bouldin:.4f}")
print(f" Calinski-Harabasz Score (Higher is Better): {calinski_harabasz:.4f}")

âœ… Optimal Number of Clusters Based on Silhouette Score: 3

ðŸ“ˆ Silhouette Score (Higher is Better): 0.4945
ðŸ“‰ Davies-Bouldin Score (Lower is Better): 0.7064
ðŸ“Š Calinski-Harabasz Score (Higher is Better): 209.4535

Evaluation of Different k ValuesÂ¶

One of the critical aspects of clustering is determining the optimal number of clusters (k).
To ensure a thorough analysis, we evaluate multiple values of k and compare them based on Silhouette Score, Davies-Bouldin Score, and Calinski-Harabasz Score.

ðŸ“‹ Table: Performance Metrics for Different k ValuesÂ¶

The following table summarizes clustering performance for different values of k.

Higher Silhouette Score and Calinski-Harabasz Score are better, while lower Davies-Bouldin Score is preferred.

InÂ [90]:
# Define a range of k values to test
k_values = range(2, 10)  # Testing k from 2 to 9

# Create an empty dictionary to store results
k_evaluation = {"Silhouette Score": [], "Davies-Bouldin Score": [], "Calinski-Harabasz Score": []}

# Iterate through different k values
for k in k_values:
    kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=k, random_state=42)
    cluster_labels = kmeans.fit_predict(df_scaled)

    # Calculate evaluation metrics
    silhouette = silhouette_score(df_scaled, cluster_labels)
    davies_bouldin = davies_bouldin_score(df_scaled, cluster_labels)
    calinski_harabasz = calinski_harabasz_score(df_scaled, cluster_labels)

    # Store results with k as index
    k_evaluation["Silhouette Score"].append(silhouette)
    k_evaluation["Davies-Bouldin Score"].append(davies_bouldin)
    k_evaluation["Calinski-Harabasz Score"].append(calinski_harabasz)

# Convert results to a DataFrame and set k as the index
k_results_df = pd.DataFrame(k_evaluation, index=k_values)
k_results_df.index.name = "k value"

k_results_df
Out[90]:
Silhouette Score Davies-Bouldin Score Calinski-Harabasz Score
k value
2 0.286085 1.421606 78.317615
3 0.362830 1.019968 113.475199
4 0.363881 1.002226 112.313393
5 0.340912 1.000993 97.823491
6 0.292335 1.141222 91.336750
7 0.303851 1.121729 96.293288
8 0.318646 0.986781 99.506324
9 0.268721 1.082660 90.792422

Analysis of Clustering Performance Across Different k ValuesÂ¶

Objective: Evaluate the performance of different k values using Silhouette Score, Davies-Bouldin Score, and Calinski-Harabasz Score to determine the optimal number of clusters.

ðŸ”¹ Silhouette Score (Higher is Better)Â¶

  • The highest Silhouette Score is observed at k = 3 (0.3628) and k = 4 (0.3638).
  • Scores decrease gradually after k = 4, suggesting that adding more clusters does not significantly improve the cluster compactness.

ðŸ”¹ Davies-Bouldin Score (Lower is Better)Â¶

  • Lowest Davies-Bouldin Score is at k = 4 (1.0022), followed closely by k = 5 (1.0009) and k = 3 (1.0199).
  • Lower scores indicate better separation between clusters, meaning k = 4 or k = 5 might be ideal.

ðŸ”¹ Calinski-Harabasz Score (Higher is Better)Â¶

  • The best Calinski-Harabasz Score occurs at k = 3 (113.47), slightly higher than k = 4 (112.31).
  • A sharp drop occurs after k = 4, suggesting k = 3 or k = 4 provide the most distinct clustering.

ðŸ”¥ ConclusionÂ¶

  • k = 3 or k = 4 appear to be the best choices based on a balance of compactness, separation, and cluster validity.
  • Since k = 3 has a slightly better silhouette score and Calinski-Harabasz score, it is likely the optimal number of clusters for this dataset.

Phase 3.2: Implementing Agglomerative (Hierarchical) ClusteringÂ¶

ObjectiveÂ¶

Now that we have K-Means clusters, we will implement Hierarchical Clustering:
Use a Dendrogram to visualize hierarchical clustering and determine the best cluster count.
Compare clustering results with K-Means to validate performance.
Evaluate clustering metrics to determine effectiveness.

StepsÂ¶

  1. Dendrogram Analysis
    • Helps identify natural cluster separation before selecting k.
  2. Agglomerative Clustering
    • Uses Ward linkage to minimize variance within clusters.
  3. Cluster Validation
    • Compare clusters with Silhouette Score, Davies-Bouldin, and Calinski-Harabasz metrics.
InÂ [Â ]:
#  Create Dendrogram
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
plt.title(" Dendrogram for Hierarchical Clustering", fontsize=14, fontweight="bold")
dendrogram = sch.dendrogram(sch.linkage(df_pca[["PCA1", "PCA2"]], method="ward"))
plt.xlabel("Data Points", fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel("Euclidean Distance", fontsize=12)
plt.grid(True, linestyle="--", alpha=0.6)
plt.show()

agg_cluster = AgglomerativeClustering(n_clusters=3, metric="euclidean", linkage="ward")
df_pca["Agglomerative_Cluster"] = agg_cluster.fit_predict(df_pca[["PCA1", "PCA2"]])

#  Visualizing Agglomerative Clustering Results
plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))
sns.scatterplot(
    x=df_pca["PCA1"], 
    y=df_pca["PCA2"], 
    hue=df_pca["Agglomerative_Cluster"], 
    palette="tab10", 
    alpha=0.8, 
    edgecolor="black"
)
plt.xlabel("PCA Component 1", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
plt.ylabel("PCA Component 2", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
plt.title(" Agglomerative Clustering (PCA Projection)", fontsize=14, fontweight="bold")
plt.legend(title="Cluster")
plt.grid(True, linestyle="--", alpha=0.5)
plt.show()

#  Clustering Performance Metrics
silhouette = silhouette_score(df_pca[["PCA1", "PCA2"]], df_pca["Agglomerative_Cluster"])
davies_bouldin = davies_bouldin_score(df_pca[["PCA1", "PCA2"]], df_pca["Agglomerative_Cluster"])
calinski_harabasz = calinski_harabasz_score(df_pca[["PCA1", "PCA2"]], df_pca["Agglomerative_Cluster"])

print(f"Silhouette Score (Higher is Better): {silhouette:.4f}")
print(f"Davies-Bouldin Score (Lower is Better): {davies_bouldin:.4f}")
print(f" Calinski-Harabasz Score (Higher is Better): {calinski_harabasz:.4f}")

ðŸ“ˆ Silhouette Score (Higher is Better): 0.4287
ðŸ“‰ Davies-Bouldin Score (Lower is Better): 0.8170
ðŸ“Š Calinski-Harabasz Score (Higher is Better): 160.9326

Phase 3.3: Implementing DBSCAN (Density-Based Clustering)Â¶

ObjectiveÂ¶

Now that we have implemented K-Means & Agglomerative Clustering, we will:
Use DBSCAN to detect dense clusters & outliers.
Compare DBSCAN performance with previous methods.
Evaluate DBSCAN using clustering metrics & visualize results.

StepsÂ¶

  1. Find the optimal eps (epsilon) value
    • Use the k-distance graph to determine the best eps threshold.
  2. Apply DBSCAN Clustering
    • Assign dense regions to clusters & mark outliers as -1.
  3. Evaluate DBSCAN Performance
    • Use Silhouette Score, Davies-Bouldin, and Calinski-Harabasz Index.
  4. Compare DBSCAN results with K-Means & Agglomerative Clustering.
InÂ [Â ]:
#  Find the optimal `eps` using k-distance graph
nearest_neighbors = NearestNeighbors(n_neighbors=5)
neighbors = nearest_neighbors.fit(df_pca[["PCA1", "PCA2"]])
distances, indices = neighbors.kneighbors(df_pca[["PCA1", "PCA2"]])

# Sort distances for k-distance plot
distances = np.sort(distances[:, -1])

#  Plot k-distance graph
plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))
plt.plot(distances, marker="o", linestyle="dashed", color="purple")
plt.xlabel("Data Points (sorted by distance)", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
plt.ylabel("5-Nearest Neighbor Distance", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
plt.title(" K-Distance Graph for Optimal `eps` Selection", fontsize=14, fontweight="bold")
plt.grid(True, linestyle="--", alpha=0.5)
plt.show()

K-Distance Graph AnalysisÂ¶

The K-Distance Graph helps determine the optimal eps value for DBSCAN by plotting the distance to the 5th nearest neighbor for each point, sorted in increasing order.

From the graph, we observe a gradual increase followed by a sharp rise around eps â‰ˆ 0.65 - 0.7. This sharp bend (elbow point) suggests that points beyond this distance are likely outliers rather than part of a dense cluster.

Thus, selecting eps = 0.65 ensures that DBSCAN correctly identifies dense clusters while minimizing noise.

InÂ [Â ]:
# Apply DBSCAN with optimal `eps` from k-distance graph
dbscan = DBSCAN(eps=0.65, min_samples=5)  # Adjusted from 0.5 to 0.65
df_pca["DBSCAN_Cluster"] = dbscan.fit_predict(df_pca[["PCA1", "PCA2"]])

#  Visualizing DBSCAN Clustering Results with Optimal `eps`
plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))
sns.scatterplot(
    x=df_pca["PCA1"], 
    y=df_pca["PCA2"], 
    hue=df_pca["DBSCAN_Cluster"], 
    palette="tab10", 
    alpha=0.8, 
    edgecolor="black"
)
plt.xlabel("PCA Component 1", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
plt.ylabel("PCA Component 2", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
plt.title(" DBSCAN Clustering (Optimized `eps`)", fontsize=14, fontweight="bold")
plt.legend(title="Cluster", loc="upper right")
plt.grid(True, linestyle="--", alpha=0.5)
plt.show()

DBSCAN Clustering Analysis (Optimized eps=0.65)Â¶

The DBSCAN clustering results show three well-defined clusters (0, 1, 2) along with some noise points (-1).

  • Cluster 0 (Green): The largest cluster, covering most data points.
  • Cluster 1 (Orange): A small, dense group in the lower-right region.
  • Cluster 2 (Red): A moderate-sized group, slightly more dispersed.
  • Outliers (-1, Blue): A few points scattered in sparse regions, correctly identified as noise.

Success:

  • The optimized eps=0.65 improved clustering, detecting multiple distinct groups.
  • Outliers are properly separated from dense regions.
  • The results differ from K-Means & Agglomerative Clustering, confirming DBSCAN's strength in handling non-spherical clusters and noise filtering.

Next Step: Compare clustering metrics and interpret business implications.

Phase 3.4: Implementing Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) ClusteringÂ¶

ObjectiveÂ¶

Now that we have tested K-Means, Agglomerative Clustering, and DBSCAN, we will:
Use GMM to identify clusters based on probability distributions.
Compare GMM with other clustering methods to determine effectiveness.
Evaluate GMM using silhouette score & other clustering metrics.

** Why Use GMM?**Â¶

  • Unlike K-Means, which assigns each point to a single cluster, GMM assigns probabilities to each cluster.
  • It is useful when clusters overlap or when data has different densities.
  • Works well when clusters are elliptical rather than spherical.

** Steps**Â¶

  1. Determine the optimal number of clusters (k)
    • Use Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC) & Akaike Information Criterion (AIC).
  2. Fit GMM Model
    • Assign each data point a probability distribution.
  3. Evaluate Performance
    • Compare silhouette score with K-Means, Agglomerative, and DBSCAN.
  4. Visualize GMM Clusters
    • Plot clusters using PCA projection.
InÂ [Â ]:
# Evaluate BIC & AIC for different values of `k`
bic_scores = []
aic_scores = []
k_values = range(2, 10)

for k in k_values:
    gmm = GaussianMixture(n_components=k, random_state=42)
    gmm.fit(df_pca[["PCA1", "PCA2"]])
    bic_scores.append(gmm.bic(df_pca[["PCA1", "PCA2"]]))
    aic_scores.append(gmm.aic(df_pca[["PCA1", "PCA2"]]))

# Plot BIC & AIC Scores
plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))
plt.plot(k_values, bic_scores, marker="o", linestyle="dashed", label="BIC Score", color="red")
plt.plot(k_values, aic_scores, marker="s", linestyle="dashed", label="AIC Score", color="blue")
plt.xlabel("Number of Clusters (k)", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
plt.ylabel("Score", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
plt.title(" GMM: Selecting Optimal k using BIC & AIC", fontsize=14, fontweight="bold")
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True, linestyle="--", alpha=0.5)
plt.show()

GMM: Selecting Optimal k Using BIC & AICÂ¶

k = 4 is the Best Number of Clusters:Â¶

  • Both AIC (Blue) and BIC (Red) scores are lowest at k=4, indicating the best fit with the simplest model.
  • After k=4, both scores increase, meaning additional clusters overfit the data without adding value.
  • BIC, which penalizes complexity more, also reaches its minimum at k=4, reinforcing that 4 clusters balance accuracy and simplicity.
InÂ [Â ]:
# Select optimal k (based on lowest BIC score)
optimal_k = k_values[bic_scores.index(min(bic_scores))]
print(f"Optimal Number of Clusters (GMM): {optimal_k}")

# Fit GMM Model
gmm = GaussianMixture(n_components=optimal_k, random_state=42)
df_pca["GMM_Cluster"] = gmm.fit_predict(df_pca[["PCA1", "PCA2"]])

# Visualizing GMM Clustering Results
plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))
sns.scatterplot(
    x=df_pca["PCA1"], 
    y=df_pca["PCA2"], 
    hue=df_pca["GMM_Cluster"], 
    palette="tab10", 
    alpha=0.8, 
    edgecolor="black"
)
plt.xlabel("PCA Component 1", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
plt.ylabel("PCA Component 2", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
plt.title(" Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) Clustering", fontsize=14, fontweight="bold")
plt.legend(title="Cluster")
plt.grid(True, linestyle="--", alpha=0.5)
plt.show()

âœ… Optimal Number of Clusters (GMM): 4
InÂ [96]:
# Clustering Performance Metrics
silhouette = silhouette_score(df_pca[["PCA1", "PCA2"]], df_pca["GMM_Cluster"])
davies_bouldin = davies_bouldin_score(df_pca[["PCA1", "PCA2"]], df_pca["GMM_Cluster"])
calinski_harabasz = calinski_harabasz_score(df_pca[["PCA1", "PCA2"]], df_pca["GMM_Cluster"])

print(f"Silhouette Score (Higher is Better): {silhouette:.4f}")
print(f"Davies-Bouldin Score (Lower is Better): {davies_bouldin:.4f}")
print(f"Calinski-Harabasz Score (Higher is Better): {calinski_harabasz:.4f}")

Silhouette Score (Higher is Better): 0.5060
Davies-Bouldin Score (Lower is Better): 0.6874
Calinski-Harabasz Score (Higher is Better): 223.9519

Phase 4: Clustering Model Comparison & Final InsightsÂ¶

ObjectiveÂ¶

Now that we have tested multiple clustering algorithms, we will:
Compare K-Means, Agglomerative, DBSCAN, and GMM side by side.
Visualize how each clustering algorithm segmented the data.
Analyze the business insights behind each cluster.
Select the final clustering model for deployment.

Clustering Models UsedÂ¶

K-Means Clustering (Centroid-based, k=3)
Agglomerative Clustering (Hierarchical-based, k=3)
DBSCAN (Density-based, eps=0.65, min_samples=5)
Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM, k=3, soft clustering)

Each model has its strengths & weaknesses. Our goal is to determine which model best fits the customer segmentation task.

InÂ [Â ]:
#  Create a figure with 4 subplots for visualizing different clustering methods
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(14,10))
fig.suptitle(" Clustering Model Comparison (PCA Projection)", fontsize=16, fontweight="bold")

# Define clustering methods and titles
clustering_methods = [
    ("K-Means Clustering", "KMeans_Cluster"),
    ("Agglomerative Clustering", "Agglomerative_Cluster"),
    ("DBSCAN Clustering", "DBSCAN_Cluster"),
    ("GMM Clustering", "GMM_Cluster"),
]

# Iterate through clustering methods and plot
for ax, (title, column) in zip(axes.flatten(), clustering_methods):
    sns.scatterplot(
        x=df_pca["PCA1"], 
        y=df_pca["PCA2"], 
        hue=df_pca[column], 
        palette="tab10", 
        alpha=0.8, 
        edgecolor="black",
        ax=ax
    )
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=13, fontweight="bold")
    ax.set_xlabel("PCA Component 1", fontsize=12)
    ax.set_ylabel("PCA Component 2", fontsize=12)
    ax.legend(title="Cluster", loc="upper right")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
InÂ [98]:
# Create a DataFrame to compare clustering performance
comparison_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Model": ["K-Means", "Agglomerative", "DBSCAN", "GMM"],
    "Silhouette Score": [
        silhouette_score(df_pca[["PCA1", "PCA2"]], df_pca["KMeans_Cluster"]),
        silhouette_score(df_pca[["PCA1", "PCA2"]], df_pca["Agglomerative_Cluster"]),
        silhouette_score(df_pca.loc[df_pca["DBSCAN_Cluster"] != -1, ["PCA1", "PCA2"]], df_pca.loc[df_pca["DBSCAN_Cluster"] != -1, "DBSCAN_Cluster"]),
        silhouette_score(df_pca[["PCA1", "PCA2"]], df_pca["GMM_Cluster"]),
    ],
    "Davies-Bouldin Score": [
        davies_bouldin_score(df_pca[["PCA1", "PCA2"]], df_pca["KMeans_Cluster"]),
        davies_bouldin_score(df_pca[["PCA1", "PCA2"]], df_pca["Agglomerative_Cluster"]),
        davies_bouldin_score(df_pca.loc[df_pca["DBSCAN_Cluster"] != -1, ["PCA1", "PCA2"]], df_pca.loc[df_pca["DBSCAN_Cluster"] != -1, "DBSCAN_Cluster"]),
        davies_bouldin_score(df_pca[["PCA1", "PCA2"]], df_pca["GMM_Cluster"]),
    ],
    "Calinski-Harabasz Score": [
        calinski_harabasz_score(df_pca[["PCA1", "PCA2"]], df_pca["KMeans_Cluster"]),
        calinski_harabasz_score(df_pca[["PCA1", "PCA2"]], df_pca["Agglomerative_Cluster"]),
        calinski_harabasz_score(df_pca.loc[df_pca["DBSCAN_Cluster"] != -1, ["PCA1", "PCA2"]], df_pca.loc[df_pca["DBSCAN_Cluster"] != -1, "DBSCAN_Cluster"]),
        calinski_harabasz_score(df_pca[["PCA1", "PCA2"]], df_pca["GMM_Cluster"]),
    ],
})
comparison_df
Out[98]:
Model Silhouette Score Davies-Bouldin Score Calinski-Harabasz Score
0 K-Means 0.494521 0.706431 209.453514
1 Agglomerative 0.428737 0.816974 160.932565
2 DBSCAN 0.357510 0.709719 50.864360
3 GMM 0.505965 0.687374 223.951882

Clustering Model Comparison AnalysisÂ¶

OverviewÂ¶

This table summarizes the performance of K-Means, Agglomerative Clustering, DBSCAN, and GMM based on three clustering evaluation metrics:

Model Silhouette Score Davies-Bouldin Score Calinski-Harabasz Score
K-Means 0.494521 0.706431 209.453514
Agglomerative 0.428737 0.816974 160.932565
DBSCAN 0.357510 0.709719 50.864360
GMM 0.505965 0.687374 223.951882

1. Silhouette Score (Higher is Better)Â¶

  • GMM (0.505965) achieves the highest silhouette score, indicating better-defined clusters with clear separation and compactness.
  • K-Means (0.494521) performs slightly worse than GMM but still shows strong cluster quality.
  • Agglomerative (0.428737) and DBSCAN (0.357510) have lower silhouette scores, suggesting overlapping or poorly separated clusters.

2. Davies-Bouldin Score (Lower is Better)Â¶

  • GMM (0.687374) achieves the lowest Davies-Bouldin score, indicating better cluster compactness and separation.
  • K-Means (0.706431) performs closely behind GMM, maintaining good cluster quality.
  • DBSCAN (0.709719) and Agglomerative (0.816974) have higher scores, indicating less compact clusters and greater overlap.

3. Calinski-Harabasz Score (Higher is Better)Â¶

  • GMM (223.951882) achieves the highest Calinski-Harabasz score, indicating well-separated clusters with high variance between clusters.
  • K-Means (209.453514) also performs strongly, suggesting good cluster separation.
  • Agglomerative (160.932565) performs moderately, while DBSCAN (50.864360) performs poorly, indicating poorly defined clusters.

Overall Analysis:Â¶

  • GMM consistently achieves the best scores across all three metrics, making it the strongest performer for this dataset.
  • K-Means performs second-best, with results close to GMM, indicating it is also a good choice.
  • Agglomerative performs moderately, showing less distinct clusters.
  • DBSCAN performs the worst, likely due to inconsistent densities or poor eps selection.

Conclusion:Â¶

  • GMM is the best clustering model for this dataset, providing well-defined, compact, and separated clusters.
  • K-Means is a strong alternative, especially if computational efficiency is required.
  • Agglomerative is less effective but may still provide useful hierarchical insights.
  • DBSCAN is unsuitable for this dataset, possibly due to inconsistent cluster densities or poor hyperparameters.

Phase 5: Business Insights & Final ConclusionsÂ¶

ObjectiveÂ¶

Now that we have determined GMM as the best clustering model, we will:
Analyze the customer segments created by GMM.
Interpret the characteristics of each cluster.
Provide business recommendations based on the segmentation.
Summarize the entire projectâ€™s findings.

Why Does Customer Segmentation Matter?Â¶

  • Personalized Marketing: Different segments have unique spending habits.
  • Optimized Customer Engagement: Businesses can tailor promotions to specific groups.
  • Strategic Decision-Making: Helps allocate resources efficiently to high-value customers.
  • Customer Retention & Acquisition: Understand customer needs to improve satisfaction.
InÂ [99]:
# Compute the average characteristics for each customer cluster
gmm_cluster_profiles = df.copy()
gmm_cluster_profiles["GMM_Cluster"] = df_pca["GMM_Cluster"]
cluster_summary = gmm_cluster_profiles.groupby("GMM_Cluster").mean()

cluster_summary
Out[99]:
Gender Age Income (k$) How Much They Spend Spending Efficiency Age-Income Ratio
GMM_Cluster
0 0.602564 49.948718 46.794872 40.923077 0.871958 0.978798
1 0.555556 28.904762 76.142857 69.714286 0.931917 2.680953
2 0.600000 25.480000 25.560000 75.320000 3.199348 1.044202
3 0.441176 41.647059 88.411765 16.882353 0.190248 2.304990

GMM Customer Segments Analysis (4 Clusters)Â¶

Cluster 0: Older Moderate SpendersÂ¶

  • Age: 49.95 years (oldest group)
  • Income: $46.79k (moderate income)
  • Spending: $40.92 (moderate spending)
  • Spending Efficiency: 0.87 (balanced spending habits)
  • Profile: Likely middle-aged, financially stable, conservative spenders.
  • Business Strategy: Offer retirement plans, family-oriented services, and loyalty programs.

Cluster 1: Younger High-Income, High-Spending CustomersÂ¶

  • Age: 28.90 years (young professionals)
  • Income: $76.14k (high income)
  • Spending: $69.71 (high spending)
  • Spending Efficiency: 0.93 (balanced spending relative to income)
  • Profile: Likely young professionals or high-earning early-career individuals.
  • Business Strategy: Provide luxury products, premium services, and aspirational branding.

Cluster 2: Young, Low-Income, High-Spending CustomersÂ¶

  • Age: 25.48 years (youngest group)
  • Income: $25.56k (lowest income)
  • Spending: $75.32 (highest spending)
  • Spending Efficiency: 3.20 (extremely high spending relative to income)
  • Profile: Likely students or young consumers overspending or relying on credit.
  • Business Strategy: Provide budget-friendly deals, installment plans, and student discounts.

Cluster 3: Wealthy, Low-Spending CustomersÂ¶

  • Age: 41.65 years (middle-aged)
  • Income: $88.41k (highest income)
  • Spending: $16.88 (lowest spending)
  • Spending Efficiency: 0.19 (extremely low spending relative to income)
  • Profile: Likely high-income savers or investors focusing on wealth growth.
  • Business Strategy: Offer investment plans, savings programs, and high-yield financial products.

Final Business RecommendationsÂ¶

  • Cluster 0 (Older Moderate Spenders): Focus on retirement services and loyalty programs.
  • Cluster 1 (Young High-Income, High-Spenders): Provide luxury experiences and exclusive deals.
  • Cluster 2 (Young Low-Income, High-Spenders): Offer installment plans and student offers.
  • Cluster 3 (Wealthy, Low-Spenders): Promote investment and premium savings plans.

Final Takeaway:Â¶

This segmentation approach empowers the business to implement targeted strategies tailored to each segment, resulting in better customer engagement and increased profitability.

Phase 6: Answering Business Questions with VisualizationsÂ¶

ObjectiveÂ¶

To effectively answer the key business questions, we will:
Visualize the distribution of clusters based on key customer attributes.
Describe the characteristics of each customer segment using data insights.
Identify which customer group requires the most attention.

InÂ [Â ]:
# Bar Plot: Number of Customers per Cluster
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
sns.countplot(x=df_pca["GMM_Cluster"], palette="Set2")
plt.xlabel("Customer Cluster", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
plt.ylabel("Number of Customers", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
plt.title(" Customer Count per Cluster", fontsize=14, fontweight="bold")
plt.grid(True, linestyle="--", alpha=0.5)
plt.show()

Analysis of Customer Count per ClusterÂ¶

Interpretation:Â¶

  • Cluster 0 (Largest Group, ~80 Customers): This cluster represents the majority of customers. Understanding their behavior is crucial as they form the biggest segment.
  • Cluster 1 (Second Largest, ~65 Customers): A slightly smaller but still significant group. Marketing efforts should also target this segment.
  • Cluster 2 (Smallest Group, ~20 Customers): A much smaller cluster, potentially representing an outlier group with distinct spending behaviors. This cluster might need further investigation.
  • Cluster 3 (~30 Customers): A moderate-sized cluster, indicating customers with potentially unique spending habits compared to Cluster 0 and 1.

Business Implications:Â¶

  • Clusters 0 and 1 should be the main focus for customer engagement strategies, as they represent most of the customer base.
  • Cluster 2 (smallest) could indicate a niche segment or outliers that may require a different business approach (e.g., specialized promotions).
  • Cluster 3 (moderate size) may exhibit distinct spending patterns and could benefit from targeted marketing campaigns.
InÂ [Â ]:
# Scatter Plot: Income vs Spending colored by Cluster
plt.figure(figsize=(9, 6))
sns.scatterplot(
    x=df["Income (k$)"], 
    y=df["How Much They Spend"], 
    hue=df_pca["GMM_Cluster"], 
    palette="tab10", 
    alpha=0.8, 
    edgecolor="black"
)
plt.xlabel("Income (k$)", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
plt.ylabel("How Much They Spend", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
plt.title(" Income vs Spending by Cluster", fontsize=14, fontweight="bold")
plt.legend(title="Cluster")
plt.grid(True, linestyle="--", alpha=0.5)
plt.show()

Analysis of Income vs Spending by ClusterÂ¶

Cluster 0 (Mid Income, Mid Spending)

  • Customers in this cluster have moderate income levels ($40k-$70k).
  • Spending is relatively consistent and moderate.
  • They could represent financially stable, cost-conscious consumers.

Cluster 1 (High Income, High Spending)

  • These customers earn higher incomes (above $60k).
  • They also spend significantly more, often reaching $100k+.
  • Likely represents high-value customers that businesses should prioritize for premium offers.

Cluster 2 (Low Income, High Spending)

  • Customers here have lower incomes (mostly below $40k).
  • However, they still spend quite a lot, sometimes reaching $90k.
  • These customers may be spending beyond their means or using credit financing.
  • Businesses could offer installment plans or loyalty discounts to retain them.

Cluster 3 (High Income, Low Spending)

  • These customers earn high incomes (above $80k).
  • However, they do not spend as much compared to their earnings.
  • They might be saving more, investing elsewhere, or are hesitant spenders.
  • Businesses should use targeted promotions to encourage more engagement.

Business ImplicationsÂ¶

Cluster 1 (High Income, High Spending) should be the focus for premium services.
Cluster 2 (Low Income, High Spending) needs financial assistance strategies.
Cluster 3 (High Income, Low Spending) requires promotional incentives to increase spending.
Cluster 0 (Balanced Customers) can be engaged through loyalty programs.

InÂ [104]:
# Use cluster_summary directly (no cluster_ranking needed)
high_spending_threshold = cluster_summary["How Much They Spend"].mean()
high_income_threshold = cluster_summary["Income (k$)"].mean()

# Function to assign categories based on spending and income
def categorize_cluster(row):
    if row["How Much They Spend"] >= high_spending_threshold and row["Income (k$)"] >= high_income_threshold:
        return "High-Value Customers"
    elif row["How Much They Spend"] >= high_spending_threshold and row["Income (k$)"] < high_income_threshold:
        return "Potential Growth Customers"
    elif row["How Much They Spend"] < high_spending_threshold and row["Income (k$)"] >= high_income_threshold:
        return "Budget-Conscious Customers"
    else:
        return "At-Risk Customers"

#  Apply categorization to cluster_summary
cluster_summary["Customer Category"] = cluster_summary.apply(categorize_cluster, axis=1)

cluster_summary
Out[104]:

GMM_Cluster

1 0.555556 28.904762 76.142857 69.714286 0.931917 2.680953 High-Value Customers
2 0.600000 25.480000 25.560000 75.320000 3.199348 1.044202 Potential Growth Customers
3 0.441176 41.647059 88.411765 16.882353 0.190248 2.304990 Budget-Conscious Customers
InÂ [105]:
# Use cluster_summary directly
numeric_cluster_summary = cluster_summary.drop(columns=["Customer Category"], errors="ignore")

# Set figure size and style
plt.figure(figsize=(14, 6))
sns.heatmap(
    numeric_cluster_summary,
    annot=True, fmt=".2f", cmap="coolwarm", linewidths=0.5
)
plt.title("Cluster Segmentation Heatmap", fontsize=14, fontweight="bold")
plt.xticks(rotation=30, ha="right")
plt.yticks(rotation=0)
plt.show()

Cluster Segmentation Heatmap AnalysisÂ¶

This heatmap provides a comparative view of key numerical attributes across the identified clusters,
helping to understand the distinct characteristics of each group.

Key ObservationsÂ¶

Cluster 0 (Older Moderate Spenders)Â¶

  • Avg Age: 49.95 (Oldest group, likely middle-aged to senior consumers)
  • Avg Income: 46.79k (Moderate income level)
  • Avg Spending: 40.92 (Moderate spending behavior)
  • Spending Efficiency: 0.87 (Low efficiency, conservative spending habits)
  • Insight:
    • These customers are stable, moderate spenders who balance their finances well.
    • They may be risk-averse and prefer security over luxury.
    • Ideal Target: Financial security, retirement planning, and family-oriented services.

Cluster 1 (Younger High-Income, High-Spenders)Â¶

  • Avg Age: 28.90 (Young professionals, early career individuals)
  • Avg Income: 76.14k (High income, second highest in dataset)
  • Avg Spending: 69.71 (Consistently high spending behavior)
  • Spending Efficiency: 0.93 (Balanced spending-to-income ratio)
  • Insight:
    • Financially successful young professionals who are not afraid to spend.
    • Likely to value premium services, travel, and high-end lifestyle products.
    • Ideal Target: Luxury goods, exclusive memberships, and aspirational branding.

Cluster 2 (Younger Budget-Conscious Consumers)Â¶

  • Avg Age: 25.48 (Youngest group in the dataset)
  • Avg Income: 25.56k (Lowest income across all clusters)
  • Avg Spending: 75.32 (Highest spending, despite low income)
  • Spending Efficiency: 3.20 (Extremely highâ€”spending much more relative to income)
  • Insight:
    • These customers spend aggressively despite low earnings, potentially overextending financially.
    • They might be dependent on credit or short-term spending habits.
    • Ideal Target: Financial literacy programs, budgeting tools, installment-based offers.

Cluster 3 (Wealthy Low Spenders)Â¶

  • Avg Income: 88.41k (Highest of all clusters)
  • Avg Spending: 16.88 (Lowest spending despite high income)
  • Spending Efficiency: 0.19 (Very lowâ€”conservative spenders)
  • Avg Age: 41.65 (Middle-aged professionals)
  • Insight:
    • Wealthy but highly frugal individuals, possibly savers and investors rather than spenders.
    • Likely to prefer financial growth over luxury purchases.
    • Ideal Target: Investment products, wealth management services, high-yield savings accounts.

Key Business InsightsÂ¶

Cluster 2 requires the most attention:

  • They are spending the most relative to their income, possibly leading to financial instability.
  • Potential target for financial planning, budget-conscious products, and credit management.

Cluster 3 represents high-income savers:

  • Ideal candidates for premium savings, investment plans, or long-term wealth-building services.

Cluster 1 is the ideal high-spending group:

  • Strong potential for luxury products, high-end services, and exclusive VIP programs.

Cluster 0 consists of older, moderate spenders:

  • Marketing strategies should focus on retirement planning, financial security, and family-friendly services.
